title: Pork Chops with Mustard Cream Sauce
subtitle: Pan-fried pork chops in a Dijon mustard and double cream sauce with shallots
excerpt: Pork chops with mustard cream sauce is a quick weeknight dish. The chops are browned hard in the pan, removed, and the sauce is built from the same pan with shallots, white wine, cream, and Dijon. The whole thing is done in 25 minutes.
sourceNotes: British and French brasserie tradition. Mustard cream sauces for pork are documented across British and French cooking from the 19th century; the pan-sauce method using wine and cream is a French technique adopted into British restaurant and home cooking in the 20th century.

recipe.temperatureNote: Hob only; no oven temperature required.

glossaryTerms.definition: To simmer a liquid uncovered until some of it evaporates, concentrating the flavour and thickening the consistency. A sauce reduced by half is roughly twice as intense as the original liquid.

content.text: Pork chops with mustard cream sauce follows the standard pan-sauce sequence: sear the protein, remove it, build the sauce in the same pan from the residue, return the protein to finish. The mustard goes in at the end so its sharpness stays fresh. The sauce is made in the same time the chops need to rest.

Season the {{pork-chop}} pork chops generously on both sides. Heat a little oil in a wide frying pan over a high heat until very hot. Add the chops and cook for 3 to 4 minutes per side until deeply browned. Remove and rest on a plate.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter, {{shallot}} shallots, and thyme. Cook for 3 minutes until the shallots are soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and stir for 30 seconds.
Pour in the {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine and {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock. Let it bubble over a medium heat, scraping up the pan residue, until

Stir in the {{double-cream}} of double cream and bring to a simmer. Return the pork chops to the pan and cook for 4 to 5 minutes, turning once, until the pork is cooked through and the sauce is thick enough to coat the back of a spoon. Remove from the heat and stir in the mustard. Season and rest for 5 minutes.

items.cause: Heat was too high when the cream went in, causing it to break.
items.fix: Keep the heat at a gentle simmer once the cream is in. If the sauce breaks, remove the pan from the heat, add a splash of cold stock, and whisk vigorously to bring it back.

items.cause: Cooked too long after the sear.
items.fix: Pork chops only need 4 to 5 minutes in the sauce after browning. Pull them the moment there is no pink remaining. They will continue to cook in the residual heat of the sauce.

items.cause: The pan residue wasn't used fully, or the wine and stock didn't reduce enough.
items.fix: Scrape the base of the pan thoroughly when the wine goes in. Let it reduce to a syrupy consistency before adding the cream.

content.text: Pork chops with a cream and mustard pan sauce belong to the tradition of French brasserie cooking that became embedded in British restaurant menus from the 1970s onward. The pan-sauce method (build the sauce in the same pan the meat was cooked in, using the residue as a flavour base) is one of the most practical in French cooking, and one of the most transferable. The mustard goes in last: cooked mustard loses its sharpness quickly, and the sauce depends on that bite.
